report:
## Your Health Assessment

Hi Jake, thanks for taking the time to complete this assessment. Your answers paint a clear picture, and there are some important connections here that we think will reframe how you see your digestive issues.

### Your Eating Pattern Is Working Against Your Gut

This one stands out immediately. Fasting most of the day and then eating a single large, high-fat meal in the evening is a significant driver of the very symptoms you want to resolve. Here's why: when you fast for extended periods, your body leans heavily on stress hormones like cortisol and adrenaline to maintain blood sugar. These hormones actively suppress digestive function, slowing motility, reducing stomach acid, and weakening enzyme secretion. Then, when you finally eat a large meal, your digestive system is essentially being asked to do its hardest job in its most compromised state. The constipation, the straining, the bloating, the fact that your symptoms show up between meals rather than right after eating... all of this fits. Your gut isn't failing you. It's being set up to fail by the pattern surrounding it.

### Signs Point to Microbial Overgrowth

Your white tongue coating is one of the most reliable informal markers of gut dysbiosis, an imbalance of bacteria or fungi in the digestive tract. Combined with your constipation, straining, and the fact that your symptoms improve after a bowel movement, this suggests that microbial overgrowth is producing irritation and gas that builds up between meals. When stool sits in the GI tract longer than it should, it feeds further bacterial fermentation, creating a cycle of bloating and discomfort. The relief you feel after a bowel movement is telling: it means the presence of stool and the microbial activity around it are actively driving your symptoms.

### The Cold Hands and Brain Fog Aren't Separate Issues

You might think of your cold extremities, brain fog, and digestive problems as unrelated. Through the bioenergetic lens, they are deeply connected. Feeling frequently cold, especially in the fingers, toes, and nose, is a classic sign of reduced thyroid function. Your thyroid is the master regulator of your metabolic rate, and it directly controls gut motility, stomach acid production, and digestive enzyme output. When thyroid activity is low, everything slows down, including your bowels. The brain fog fits here too. Low energy metabolism means less fuel for the brain, and the gut inflammation from dysbiosis can produce compounds like endotoxin and excess serotonin that further cloud cognitive function. Your 6/10 energy level, your constipation, your cold hands, and your mental fog are not four separate problems. They are likely one pattern expressing itself in different places.

### Stress Hormones Are Running the Show

Prolonged fasting keeps cortisol and adrenaline elevated for hours. These hormones suppress gut motility, impair immune surveillance in the intestinal lining, and make the gut more vulnerable to bacterial overgrowth. They also promote the very between-meal symptoms you're experiencing. Your body is spending its energy on stress adaptation rather than on digestion and repair. This creates a reinforcing loop: stress suppresses digestion, poor digestion creates more systemic stress, and the cycle continues.

---

A quiz like this can surface patterns, but it can only go so far. Your answers suggest that your bloating and digestive issues are connected to deeper metabolic and stress-related dynamics that a conversation can explore much more precisely.

If you'd like to dig into what's driving these patterns in your specific case, we offer a completely free consultation with someone on our team who understands this framework inside and out. No cost, no obligation, just a real conversation about your health. You can book using the button below.
